text: We demonstrate our technique of robotic pancreatic or duodenectomy.
The patient is positioned supine in a reversed Trendelenburg position with a slight left tilt, preparing for the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The robotic surgical cart is positioned head-on to the patient, ready for the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
A 12mm camera port is inserted in the left pararectal line at the level of the umbilicus, providing a view for the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgeon discusses the alternative placement of the camera port to the right of the umbilicus, which provides improved visualization of the uncinate process, supra-amascentric vessels, and portal vein.
The surgeon places the remaining 8mm ports, a 12mm port, and a 5mm assistant port in the abdominal area, following the established port placement strategy for the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon lifts the stomach cranially and performs dissection of the gastrocolic ligament, carefully preserving the short gastric vessels visible during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the right gastroepiploic artery approximately one centimeter from its origin and divides it, using precise surgical techniques to separate the artery from surrounding tissues.
The majority of the retropancreatic lymph nodes remain attached to the specimen block during the dissection, which is relevant for pancreaticoduodenectomy procedures.
The surgeon begins tunneling around the portal vein, carefully creating a passage through the surrounding tissue to expose the vein for further dissection.
The surgeon explores the confluence of the superior mesenteric vein with the portal vein at the neck of the pancreas, following the middle colic and gastroepiploic vessels.
The surgeon uses harmonic shears and a suction cannula to perform blunt dissection, continuing the separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
This classically described step should be done only if it is easy to do so.
The surgeon develops the tunnel progressively through the pancreatic parenchyma, carefully navigating around inflammatory adhesions.
The surgeon transects the stomach proximal to the antrum using endo-GIA staplers, visibly separating the gastric tissue.
The surgeon visually inspects the gastroduodenal artery stump, which is a potential source of post-operative complications, ensuring it is properly managed to prevent future issues.
The surgeon dissects the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) and applies vascular clips before dividing the artery.
The surgeon considers suturing or ligating the structure, and if there is sufficient space, vascular staplers may be utilized to secure it.
The surgeon carefully identifies small tributaries of the portal vein, ensuring precise dissection to avoid damage to these delicate vessels.
The surgeon sutures and divides small tributary veins before proceeding to approach the gastroduodenal artery.
The surgeon performs a wide takedown of the hepatic colonic flexure, extending the dissection medially to the origin of the right colonic vessels and sometimes down to the cecum.
The surgeon performs an extensive caulker maneuver, manipulating the surrounding tissues to facilitate further dissection and exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon detaches the head of the pancreas from the retroperitoneal space, facilitating a safe and easier dissection of the uncinate process.
The surgeon identifies the left side of the aorta as the main landmark for the lateral edge of the dissection during the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the left renal vein and the origin of the superior mesenteric artery as landmarks for completing the extended Cocker maneuver during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon harvests enlarged lymph nodes from the inter-aortocaval space during the lymphadenectomy, carefully excising these nodes from the surrounding tissue.
This step is also a part of evaluation of resectability.
The surgeon performs the division of the duodenal-jejunal flexure and executes a right-sided D-rotation of the duodenum, preparing for the transduction of the neck of the pancreas.
The surgeon extensively mobilizes the fourth part of the duodenum, carefully manipulating the tissue to achieve optimal exposure for the subsequent steps of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon divides the first jejunal loop using endogia staplers, visibly separating the intestinal segment.
The surgeon performs an enterocrate cholecystectomy, dissecting down to the cystic duct, with visible separation of the gallbladder from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the neck of the pancreas anteriorly to the portal vein using harmonic shears, carefully separating the pancreatic tissue from the underlying vascular structures.
The surgeon achieves optimal exposure of the inferior and superior margins of the pancreatic neck, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
After the pancreatic transduction, the surgeon identifies the pancreatic duct and cannulates it using an endoluminal stent, ensuring precise placement within the duct.
The surgeon assesses the pancreatic duct for inadvertent sealing due to harmonic thermal lateral spread, preparing to reopen and stent the duct if necessary.
The surgeon performs dissection of the uncinate process using a right to left approach, progressing from below upwards, carefully separating the tissue from surrounding structures.

The surgeon discusses alternative approaches for dissection, including the SMA first approach, where the superior mesenteric artery is dissected at its origin, and the uncinate first approach, where the neck of the pancreas and duodenum are resected following the dissection of the uncinate process.
The surgeon controls bleeding by applying hemostatic clips and suturing techniques to the affected area.
Clips, however, have been superior in our experience.
The surgeon controls bleeding from Henle's trunk using hemostatic clips and then divides the vessel with a harmonic scalpel.
It is important to ensure complete hemostasis here, as retroportal lamina is the most common site of postoperative hemorrhage.
The surgeon retracts the superior mesenteric vein to the left using a suction cannula, enhancing visualization of the surgical field posterior to the vein.
The suction cannula is used to retract the superior mesenteric vein to the left, maintaining a clear surgical field during the dissection.
The surgeon loops the superior mesenteric vein with a vascular loop and retracts it to the left, enhancing visualization of the surgical field posterior to the vein.
Special attention needs to be given to the inferior pancreatic or duodenal artery.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to circumferentially dissect the artery, followed by the application of hemostatic clips to secure the vessel.
This step helps in avoiding the retraction of the artery as well as torrential bleeding, which would be difficult to control.
We may encounter unusual complex situations during the dissection year like bulky tumors of pancreatic head which shifts the confluence of superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein to the left.

Other complex situations may arise due to anatomical variations, chronic inflammation due to chronic pancreatitis, bleeding and vascular involvement of superior mesenteric vein or superior mesenteric artery.
Additional advantage of robot can be seen in cases having vascular involvement of superior mesenteric artery and superior mesenteric vein.
The robotic arms provide stable retraction, enhancing dexterity and precision during the repair and suturing of vessels, with visible control over the surgical field.
During the dissection, the surgeon identifies vascular abnormalities, including a replaced right hepatic artery type III, which is visible in the surgical field.
Others include accessory right hepatic artery and replaced celiac trunk.
The surgeon performs dissection along the uncinate process, dividing the tissue into three distinct imaginary layers for precise separation.
The surgeon clips and cuts two to three tributaries within the anterior portal layer during the dissection along the uncinate process.
The surgeon identifies the arterial layer, which includes the inferior and superior pancreaticoduodenal arteries, during the dissection along the uncinate process.

The surgeon addresses the posterior lymphatic layer using a harmonic scalpel, performing dissection along the superior mesenteric vein with hemostatic clips and harmonic to prevent bleeding. The common bile duct is divided using cold scissors if thin and small, or diathermy if thick and fibrotic.
The surgeon identifies and divides the common bile duct cranially to the origin of the cystic duct, ensuring precise separation of these structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the pancreaticoduodenectomy specimen and places it into an endobag for removal.
The surgical team begins the reconstruction phase following the completion of the dissection and specimen removal.
The surgeon begins the reconstructive phase, considering variations in pancreatic duct size, pancreatic parenchyma quality, bile duct size, and pylorus preservation, similar to the open approach.
The surgeon passes a jejunal loop through the mesocolic route, where the fourth part of the duodenum normally crosses, and positions it in the upper quadrants for digestive reconstruction.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepaticojejunostomy, connecting the jejunal loop to the hepatic duct.
The surgeon uses a running suture with 4-0 or 5-0 PDS or Vicryl for the posterior row, while placing interrupted stitches in the anterior row during the reconstruction phase.
The surgeon reconstructs the anterior layer by placing stitches one millimeter apart, starting from the right side of the duct.
The surgeon uses a running suture for the anterior row of the bile duct reconstruction, accommodating the visual characteristics of a very dilated and thick bile duct.
Multiple options are available to perform the pancreatic ojejunostomy.
The robotic approach guarantees the same technical level of sophistication of the labratomic strategy, adding scaling effect and magnified view.
In this way, the operative setting is very similar to the use of a surgical microscope platform.
The main factors influencing the kind of pancreatic anastomosis to be performed are the pancreatic parenchyma texture,
and the pancreatic duct diameter.
If the pancreatic tissue is fragile or soft or the duct is very small less than three millimeters, a gland to mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy is the preferred option.
Conversely, in case of firm or fibrotic pancreas and for duct more than four millimeters, a duct to mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy is the best choice.
The surgeon anchors the posterior capsule of the pancreatic stump to the jejunal serosa using polypropylene sutures, securing the connection between these structures.
The surgeon creates a small opening in the jejunal mucosa and performs an anastomosis of the pancreatic duct using interrupted PDS sutures.
A small stent is placed inside the pancreatic duct, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 polypropylene or PDS suture to secure the anterior aspect of the pancreatic capsule to the jejunal serosa.
The surgeon adds additional interrupted stitches to reinforce the suture between the pancreatic duct and the jejunal mucosa, ensuring a secure anastomosis.
If the capsule of the pancreas is fragile, a Blomgaard's technique can be used to anchor the pancreas to the jejunal loop.
The surgeon performs a gastrojejunostomy, creating an anastomosis between the stomach and the jejunum.
The surgeon places the pancreatic anastomosis on the same jejunal loop as the biliary anastomosis, positioned forty centimeters distal to the biliary connection.

The surgeon performs an omega loop gastrojejunal anastomosis using inter-GIA staplers. The enterotomy site is closed with continuous sutures, either vicryl or PDS-III-Zero, after inserting a nasojejunal tube into the jejunal limb.
The surgeon extracts the specimen from the surgical field using an endoback, pulling it through a small upper midline incision.
Three surgical drains are positioned: one adjacent to the pancreatic anastomosis, another near the biliary anastomosis, and the third in the pelvic region.
Thank you.
